blocks.body: May / Might shows possibility, probability, and more formal ways to give or ask for permission.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"In this topic you will see how may and might help you talk about possibility, probability, and more formal permission.","rules":[{"label":"POSSIBILITY","color":"emerald","text":"May and might show that something is possible but not certain.","example":"She may come later. / He might be busy."},{"label":"FORMAL PERMISSION","color":"blue","text":"May often appears in more formal requests and rules.","example":"May I ask a question? / Students may leave early."},{"label":"NUANCE","color":"amber","text":"Might often sounds a little more cautious or less certain than may.","example":"It may rain. / It might rain."}]}

{"title":"1. Key forms","intro":"After may and might, we also use the base verb without to.","items":[{"label":"Possibility","title":"may + V","subtitle":"may arrive / may not know"},{"label":"Tentative possibility","title":"might + V","subtitle":"might arrive / might not know"},{"label":"Permission","title":"May I + V?","subtitle":"May I come in? / May I ask something?"},{"label":"Spelling alert","title":"may be vs maybe","subtitle":"He may be late. / Maybe he is late."}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"Possibility and probability","color":"emerald","description":"Use may and might when something is possible but not certain.","examples":[{"en":"She may be at home now.","ua":"She may be at home now."},{"en":"We might miss the bus if we wait here.","ua":"We might miss the bus if we wait here."},{"en":"It may take longer than we think.","ua":"It may take longer than we think."}]},{"label":"Permission","color":"blue","description":"May often appears in more formal permission and official rules.","examples":[{"en":"May I sit here?","ua":"May I sit here?"},{"en":"You may use a dictionary during the test.","ua":"You may use a dictionary during the test."},{"en":"Visitors may enter after 10 a.m.","ua":"Visitors may enter after 10 a.m."}],"note":"In everyday speech, people also use can very often for permission."},{"label":"May or might?","color":"amber","description":"In many sentences, may and might are very close in meaning. Might often sounds a bit more cautious or tentative.","examples":[{"en":"I may go out later.","ua":"I may go out later."},{"en":"I might go out later.","ua":"I might go out later."},{"en":"He may know the answer, but he might stay quiet.","ua":"He may know the answer, but he might stay quiet."}],"note":"The difference is not always large, but might often sounds more tentative."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Mixing up may be and maybe.","wrong":"Maybe be late.","right":"✅ He may be late. / Maybe he is late.","hint":"May be = modal + verb, while maybe = adverb."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using to after the modal.","wrong":"She might to come later.","right":"✅ She might come later.","hint":"After may / might, use the bare infinitive."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Adding an ending to the main verb.","wrong":"He may comes tomorrow.","right":"✅ He may come tomorrow.","hint":"After a modal verb, the main verb does not change form."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["May and might show possibility without full certainty.","May appears more often in formal permission: May I...?.","Might often sounds more cautious or less direct than may.","After may / might, use the base verb without to.","Remember the difference: may be = modal + verb, maybe = adverb."]}
